title: Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD) in a 34-Year-Old Combat Veteran Presenting to Outpatient Mental Health: Recognizing the Four DSM-5 Symptom Clusters, Screening for Suicide/Substance-Use Comorbidity, Delivering TRAUMA-INFORMED Care, Teaching GROUNDING/Coping for Flashbacks and Hyperarousal, and Connecting to First-Line Trauma-Focused Psychotherapy and SSRI/Prazosin (NOT Long-Term Benzodiazepines)
subtitle: POST-TRAUMATIC STRESS DISORDER (PTSD) develops after EXPOSURE to actual or threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violence — by directly experiencing it, witnessing it, learning it happened to a close family member/friend, or repeated/extreme exposure to aversive details (e.g., first responders, combat). DSM-5 describes FOUR SYMPTOM CLUSTERS: (1) INTRUSION — recurrent involuntary distressing MEMORIES, NIGHTMARES, FLASHBACKS (dissociative reliving as if the event is happening again), and intense psychological/physiological distress to internal or external CUES/TRIGGERS that resemble the trauma; (2) AVOIDANCE — persistent effort to avoid trauma-related thoughts/feelings AND external reminders (people, places, conversations, activities, situations); (3) NEGATIVE ALTERATIONS in COGNITION and MOOD — inability to recall key features of the event, persistent negative beliefs about oneself/others/the world, distorted blame or GUILT, persistent negative emotional state (fear, horror, anger, shame), markedly diminished interest (anhedonia), feeling DETACHED/ESTRANGED from others, and an inability to experience positive emotions; (4) ALTERATIONS in AROUSAL and REACTIVITY — irritability/angry outbursts, reckless or self-destructive behavior, HYPERVIGILANCE, an exaggerated STARTLE response, problems with CONCENTRATION, and SLEEP disturbance. By definition the symptoms last MORE THAN 1 MONTH and cause significant DISTRESS or functional IMPAIRMENT (work, relationships); when the same picture lasts LESS than 1 month after the trauma it is ACUTE STRESS DISORDER. A dissociative subtype (depersonalization/derealization) may occur. ASSESSMENT + SAFETY: PTSD carries HIGH COMORBIDITY with DEPRESSION, SUBSTANCE/ALCOHOL USE DISORDER (often self-medication), and SUICIDE — so the nurse SCREENS for suicidal/self-harm ideation, substance use, and CURRENT SAFETY (e.g., ongoing abuse/intimate-partner violence) FIRST, and may use validated tools (e.g., the PCL-5). TRAUMA-INFORMED CARE is the framework for ALL interactions (SAMHSA principles: SAFETY; TRUSTWORTHINESS/transparency; PEER support; COLLABORATION/mutuality; EMPOWERMENT, VOICE and CHOICE; and cultural/historical/gender sensitivity) — it shifts the question from 'what is WRONG with you?' to 'what HAPPENED to you?', actively AVOIDS RE-TRAUMATIZATION, gives the client CONTROL and CHOICE, provides PREDICTABILITY (explain before doing, respect personal space, avoid surprising or cornering), and minimizes coercive/forced interventions. THERAPEUTIC COMMUNICATION: build TRUST with a calm, nonjudgmental, accepting presence; VALIDATE feelings; let the client set the PACE and do NOT force them to recount the traumatic event in detail before they are ready (a forced, premature trauma narrative can re-traumatize); do NOT MINIMIZE ('at least you survived,' 'just move on') or claim to know exactly how they feel. For FLASHBACKS, dissociation, or acute anxiety, teach and use GROUNDING techniques — the 5-4-3-2-1 sensory method, paced/diaphragmatic breathing, and ORIENTING to the present ('you are safe, you are here, it is [today], the event is over') — to reconnect the client to the here-and-now. TREATMENT: FIRST-LINE is trauma-focused PSYCHOTHERAPY — Trauma-Focused CBT, Prolonged Exposure (PE), Cognitive Processing Therapy (CPT), and Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR). First-line PHARMACOLOGY is an SSRI (sertraline, paroxetine — FDA-approved) or SNRI (venlafaxine); PRAZOSIN (an alpha-1 antagonist) is used for trauma-related NIGHTMARES and sleep disturbance. BENZODIAZEPINES are generally AVOIDED — they do NOT treat the core disorder, carry dependence/misuse risk (especially with comorbid substance use disorder), can impair the processing/extinction central to recovery, and may WORSEN outcomes; alcohol/illicit substances should be reduced (they worsen symptoms and sleep). HEALTH PROMOTION/RECOVERY: provide PSYCHOEDUCATION that normalizes symptoms as an understandable RESPONSE to trauma (reducing shame/self-blame and supporting engagement), encourage SLEEP hygiene, regular EXERCISE, SOCIAL/PEER support (e.g., veterans' groups), stress-management/relaxation skills, limiting alcohol/caffeine, and gradual return to function; screen at-risk populations (veterans, abuse/assault survivors, first responders) and connect to RESOURCES and follow-up. NURSING ROLE: recognize the four symptom clusters; screen for safety/suicide/substance comorbidity FIRST; deliver trauma-informed, nonjudgmental care that gives control/choice and avoids re-traumatization; teach grounding/coping for flashbacks and hyperarousal and stabilize sleep; facilitate evidence-based treatment (trauma-focused psychotherapy +/- SSRI/SNRI, prazosin for nightmares — NOT long-term benzodiazepines); and provide psychoeducation, peer support, and recovery-oriented follow-up.

patient.name: Marcus R.
patient.code: Outpatient mental-health care; goal = establish safety, build a trauma-informed therapeutic relationship, teach grounding/coping, and connect to evidence-based treatment and recovery resources for PTSD
patient.admitDate: A 34-year-old combat veteran (separated from service ~14 months ago) presents to the outpatient mental-health/primary-care clinic at his partner's urging. For several months he has had recurrent NIGHTMARES and intrusive memories of a combat event, FLASHBACKS triggered by loud noises and crowds, marked AVOIDANCE (won't drive on highways, avoids news/crowds), HYPERVIGILANCE with an exaggerated startle, irritability, poor concentration, and severe SLEEP disturbance, along with detachment from his family and persistent guilt. Symptoms have lasted more than a month and are impairing his work and relationships. He is being assessed for PTSD with attention to SAFETY (suicide/self-harm and alcohol use), with trauma-informed care, grounding/coping teaching, and connection to trauma-focused therapy and appropriate medication.
patient.room: Outpatient mental-health clinic — quiet, low-stimulation room with the door accessible and seating arranged so the client is not cornered; interdisciplinary team (psychiatry/mental-health prescriber, mental-health nurse, trauma therapist, peer-support specialist, social work); veterans' resources and follow-up being coordinated

vitals.temp: 37.0°C; appears tense, guarded, scanning the room (HYPERVIGILANCE); reports recurrent nightmares, intrusive memories, flashbacks to loud noises/crowds, avoidance, irritability, poor concentration, and severe insomnia for >1 month with impaired work/relationships — consistent with PTSD

37.0°C; SAFETY screened first given high comorbidity: assessed for SUICIDAL/self-harm ideation and plan, ALCOHOL/substance use (self-medication), and current safety (e.g., access to firearms, intimate-partner violence); validated screening (e.g., PCL-5) and risk assessment guide the plan

37.1°C; a sudden loud noise in the hallway triggers a FLASHBACK — he becomes diaphoretic, tremulous, and momentarily disconnected from the present (dissociative reliving), with tachycardia, hypertension, and tachypnea; needs calm, present-focused grounding (NOT confrontation or restraint)

37.0°C; nurse uses GROUNDING — a calm voice, orienting him ('you are safe, you are here in the clinic, it is [today], the event is over'), the 5-4-3-2-1 sensory technique, and paced/diaphragmatic breathing; he reconnects to the present, the flashback resolves, and vitals trend down

37.0°C; collaborative, trauma-informed plan: referral to trauma-focused PSYCHOTHERAPY (CPT/PE/EMDR), first-line SSRI (sertraline) with PRAZOSIN for nightmares, psychoeducation, peer/veterans' support, sleep and alcohol counseling — and explicitly AVOIDING long-term benzodiazepines; he is given choices and control over the pace

37.0°C; over weeks of therapy + medication: improved sleep, fewer nightmares/intrusions, less avoidance, better concentration, and reconnection with family; engaged in a veterans' peer group; reduced alcohol use; calm, future-oriented — recovery trajectory

labs.n: PRESENTATION + recognizing PTSD (DSM-5 four symptom clusters + timeframe)
labs.v: Marcus R. is a 34-year-old combat VETERAN presenting with several months of recurrent NIGHTMARES and intrusive memories, FLASHBACKS triggered by loud noises and crowds, marked AVOIDANCE (won't drive highways, avoids news/crowds/reminders), HYPERVIGILANCE with an exaggerated STARTLE response, irritability, poor concentration, severe SLEEP disturbance, detachment from his family, and persistent GUILT — present for MORE THAN 1 MONTH with clear functional impairment. This fits POST-TRAUMATIC STRESS DISORDER (PTSD): a disorder that develops after EXPOSURE to actual/threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violence (experienced, witnessed, learned of in a close other, or via repeated/extreme exposure to aversive details). DSM-5 organizes symptoms into FOUR CLUSTERS: (1) INTRUSION — distressing memories, nightmares, flashbacks (dissociative reliving), and intense distress/physiologic reactivity to trauma CUES; (2) AVOIDANCE — of trauma-related thoughts/feelings and external reminders; (3) NEGATIVE alterations in COGNITION/MOOD — distorted blame/guilt, persistent negative beliefs, anhedonia, detachment/estrangement, inability to feel positive emotions; (4) AROUSAL/REACTIVITY — irritability/anger, recklessness, hypervigilance, exaggerated startle, concentration and sleep problems. The >1-MONTH timeframe distinguishes PTSD from ACUTE STRESS DISORDER (same picture but lasting <1 month after the trauma). The nurse's priorities: recognize the clusters, screen for SAFETY first, deliver trauma-informed care, teach grounding/coping, and connect him to evidence-based treatment.
labs.r: PTSD = post-trauma disorder with FOUR DSM-5 clusters — INTRUSION (memories/nightmares/FLASHBACKS/cue reactivity), AVOIDANCE (of reminders), NEGATIVE COGNITION/MOOD (guilt, negative beliefs, detachment, anhedonia), and AROUSAL (HYPERVIGILANCE, exaggerated startle, irritability, sleep/concentration problems), lasting >1 MONTH with impairment (<1 month = acute stress disorder). RN role: recognize the clusters.

labs.n: Assessment + SAFETY first (suicide / substance comorbidity / current safety)
labs.v: SAFETY is assessed FIRST because PTSD carries HIGH COMORBIDITY: major DEPRESSION, SUBSTANCE and ALCOHOL USE DISORDER (frequently as self-medication for intrusion/sleep symptoms), and an elevated risk of SUICIDE and self-harm. The nurse SCREENS for SUICIDAL/self-harm IDEATION, intent, and plan; for ALCOHOL/substance use; and for CURRENT SAFETY — including access to lethal means (e.g., FIREARMS, especially relevant for veterans) and any ongoing danger such as intimate-partner violence. Validated tools support assessment (e.g., the PCL-5 for PTSD symptom severity, depression and substance screens, and a structured suicide-risk assessment). A positive suicide screen drives immediate safety planning, means-restriction counseling, and an appropriate level of care. The HISTORY is gathered in a trauma-informed way — letting the client share at HIS pace, NOT forcing a detailed trauma narrative before he is ready (which can re-traumatize), and focusing on current symptoms, function, triggers, sleep, substance use, supports, and safety. THIS VETERAN is screened for suicide and alcohol use and for firearm access and current safety, with results guiding the plan, before deeper trauma-processing work begins.
labs.r: SAFETY FIRST — PTSD has HIGH comorbidity with depression, substance/ALCOHOL use disorder (self-medication), and SUICIDE/self-harm. Screen for suicidal ideation/plan, substance use, and current safety (lethal means/firearms, intimate-partner violence); use validated tools (PCL-5, suicide-risk assessment). Gather history at the client's pace without forcing a premature trauma narrative. RN role: screen safety + comorbidity before trauma-processing work.

labs.v: TRAUMA-INFORMED CARE (TIC) is the framework for EVERY interaction. The SAMHSA principles are SAFETY (physical + emotional); TRUSTWORTHINESS and transparency; PEER support; COLLABORATION and mutuality (sharing power); EMPOWERMENT, VOICE, and CHOICE; and attention to CULTURAL, historical, and gender issues. Practically, TIC shifts the frame from 'what is WRONG with you?' to 'what HAPPENED to you?', actively works to AVOID RE-TRAUMATIZATION, and gives the client CONTROL: explain procedures BEFORE doing them, offer CHOICES, provide PREDICTABILITY, respect personal SPACE, avoid SURPRISING or CORNERING the client, keep an accessible exit, and minimize coercive/forced interventions. THERAPEUTIC COMMUNICATION: establish TRUST with a calm, nonjudgmental, accepting presence; use active listening and VALIDATE his experience and feelings; let HIM set the PACE; do NOT pressure him to recount the trauma in detail before he is ready; and AVOID minimizing statements ('at least you made it,' 'just move on') or false reassurance ('I know exactly how you feel'). For a FLASHBACK, dissociation, or acute anxiety, use GROUNDING to reconnect him to the present: speak calmly and ORIENT him ('you are SAFE, you are HERE in the clinic, it is [today], that event is in the PAST'), use the 5-4-3-2-1 SENSORY method (name 5 things you see, 4 you hear, 3 you can touch, 2 you smell, 1 you taste), and coach PACED/diaphragmatic BREATHING — NOT confrontation, restraint, or 'snapping him out of it.' Teach him to recognize early cues and self-apply grounding and breathing between visits. THIS VETERAN had a flashback in clinic that resolved with calm orientation, 5-4-3-2-1 grounding, and paced breathing.
labs.r: TRAUMA-INFORMED CARE (SAMHSA: safety, trust/transparency, peer support, collaboration, empowerment/CHOICE, cultural sensitivity) frames every interaction — ask 'what HAPPENED to you?', avoid RE-TRAUMATIZATION, give control/choice, explain before doing, respect space, don't surprise/corner. Communication: nonjudgmental, validate, let him set the pace, don't force the trauma narrative, don't minimize. FLASHBACK → GROUNDING (orient to present, 5-4-3-2-1, paced breathing), not confrontation/restraint. RN role: trauma-informed relationship + grounding.

labs.n: Treatment — trauma-focused psychotherapy (first-line) + pharmacology + AVOID benzodiazepines
labs.v: EVIDENCE-BASED TREATMENT: FIRST-LINE is trauma-focused PSYCHOTHERAPY — Trauma-Focused CBT, PROLONGED EXPOSURE (PE), COGNITIVE PROCESSING THERAPY (CPT), and EYE MOVEMENT DESENSITIZATION AND REPROCESSING (EMDR); these have the strongest evidence and address the core disorder. PHARMACOLOGY (often combined with therapy): FIRST-LINE medications are SSRIs — SERTRALINE and PAROXETINE are FDA-approved for PTSD — or an SNRI such as VENLAFAXINE; these take several weeks for full effect, so set expectations and reinforce adherence. PRAZOSIN (an alpha-1 adrenergic antagonist) specifically targets trauma-related NIGHTMARES and sleep disturbance (monitor for orthostatic hypotension/dizziness, especially with the first dose). BENZODIAZEPINES are GENERALLY AVOIDED in PTSD: they do NOT treat the core disorder, carry significant DEPENDENCE/misuse risk (a major concern given the high rate of comorbid substance use disorder), can interfere with the emotional processing/extinction that recovery depends on, and are associated with WORSE outcomes; they are not a substitute for therapy. The nurse also counsels REDUCING ALCOHOL and illicit substances, which worsen symptoms, sleep, and risk. Care is COLLABORATIVE — the client is given information and CHOICE about therapy and medication options, consistent with trauma-informed, empowerment-based care. THIS VETERAN was referred for trauma-focused therapy (e.g., CPT) and started on sertraline with prazosin for nightmares, with benzodiazepines explicitly avoided.
labs.r: Treatment: FIRST-LINE = trauma-focused PSYCHOTHERAPY (TF-CBT, Prolonged Exposure, CPT, EMDR). First-line MEDS = SSRI (sertraline/paroxetine — FDA-approved) or SNRI (venlafaxine); takes weeks → reinforce adherence. PRAZOSIN for trauma NIGHTMARES/sleep (watch orthostasis). AVOID benzodiazepines — don't treat core PTSD, dependence/misuse risk (esp. with comorbid SUD), may worsen outcomes; reduce alcohol/substances. Collaborative, choice-based. RN role: facilitate therapy + appropriate meds, avoid benzos.

labs.n: Health promotion / recovery; Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors
labs.v: HEALTH PROMOTION + RECOVERY are central to nursing care: provide PSYCHOEDUCATION that NORMALIZES symptoms as an understandable RESPONSE to trauma (not weakness or a character flaw) — this reduces SHAME and self-blame and improves treatment ENGAGEMENT; teach SLEEP hygiene; encourage regular EXERCISE/physical activity; promote SOCIAL connection and PEER support (e.g., veterans' support groups, which leverage shared experience); teach relaxation/stress-management and the grounding/breathing skills for ongoing use; counsel LIMITING ALCOHOL and CAFFEINE (which worsen sleep/arousal); and support a gradual RETURN to valued activities, work, and relationships. SCREEN at-risk populations routinely — veterans, survivors of abuse/assault/intimate-partner violence, first responders, and people after serious accidents/disasters — and CONNECT clients to RESOURCES (mental-health treatment, peer support, veterans' services, crisis lines) with structured FOLLOW-UP. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): the nurse did NOT force a detailed trauma narrative at first contact (which can re-traumatize); did NOT minimize/dismiss his symptoms or tell him to 'just move on'; did NOT start or rely on long-term benzodiazepines as primary treatment; did NOT encourage alcohol to cope; and did NOT surprise/corner him or remove his sense of control. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: a supportive partner who encouraged help-seeking; engagement in care; access to evidence-based treatment (trauma-focused therapy + appropriate medication); peer/veterans' support; reduced alcohol use; effective coping/grounding skills; safety planning and means-restriction; and recovery-oriented, trauma-informed, nonjudgmental care. EXPECTED outcome: with a trusting therapeutic relationship, safety, grounding/coping skills, trauma-focused therapy, and appropriate medication, symptoms decrease, sleep and function improve, and the client reconnects with his life and supports. NURSING ROLE: recognize PTSD; screen safety/comorbidity first; deliver trauma-informed care; teach grounding/coping and stabilize sleep; connect to evidence-based treatment; and provide psychoeducation, peer support, and recovery-oriented follow-up.
labs.r: HEALTH PROMOTION/RECOVERY: PSYCHOEDUCATION normalizing symptoms (reduces shame, boosts engagement), sleep hygiene, exercise, SOCIAL/PEER support (veterans' groups), relaxation/grounding skills, limit alcohol/caffeine, gradual return to function; SCREEN at-risk groups + connect to resources/follow-up. NEGATIVES (correct practice): no forced trauma narrative, no minimizing, no long-term benzos, no alcohol coping, no surprising/cornering. Protective: support, engagement, evidence-based treatment, peer support, coping skills, safety planning. RN role: psychoeducation + recovery support + follow-up.

nursesNote: Outpatient mental-health clinic — Marcus R., 34, combat VETERAN (separated ~14 months ago), here at partner's urging. Several months of recurrent NIGHTMARES + intrusive memories, FLASHBACKS triggered by loud noises/crowds, marked AVOIDANCE (no highway driving, avoids news/crowds), HYPERVIGILANCE + exaggerated startle, irritability, poor concentration, severe INSOMNIA, detachment from family, persistent GUILT — >1 MONTH with impaired work/relationships → PTSD (>1 month vs <1 month = acute stress disorder). FOUR DSM-5 clusters present: intrusion, avoidance, negative cognition/mood, arousal/reactivity. SAFETY SCREENED FIRST (high comorbidity): suicidal/self-harm ideation/plan, ALCOHOL/substance use (self-medication), lethal-means access (FIREARMS) + current safety/IPV; validated tools (PCL-5, suicide-risk assessment) guide the plan. History gathered at HIS pace — did NOT force a detailed trauma narrative (re-traumatizing). TRAUMA-INFORMED CARE throughout (safety, trust, peer support, collaboration, empowerment/CHOICE, cultural sensitivity): 'what HAPPENED to you?' not 'what's wrong?'; explain before doing; offer choices; respect space; accessible exit; don't surprise/corner; minimize coercion. Communication: calm, nonjudgmental, validating; no minimizing ('just move on') or false reassurance. FLASHBACK in clinic (loud hallway noise → diaphoretic, tremulous, tachycardic/hypertensive, momentarily dissociated) RESOLVED with GROUNDING: calm orientation ('you are safe, here, it is [today], the event is over'), 5-4-3-2-1 sensory method, paced/diaphragmatic breathing — NOT confrontation/restraint; taught him to self-apply between visits. TREATMENT (collaborative, choice-based): referral to trauma-focused PSYCHOTHERAPY (CPT/PE/EMDR — first-line); first-line SSRI (SERTRALINE; or paroxetine/venlafaxine — weeks to effect, reinforce adherence); PRAZOSIN for trauma NIGHTMARES/sleep (watch orthostasis); AVOID long-term BENZODIAZEPINES (don't treat core PTSD, dependence/misuse risk esp. with comorbid SUD, may worsen outcomes); counsel reducing ALCOHOL/caffeine. HEALTH PROMOTION/RECOVERY: psychoeducation normalizing symptoms (reduce shame, improve engagement), sleep hygiene, exercise, SOCIAL/PEER support (veterans' group), relaxation/grounding skills, gradual return to function; routine screening of at-risk groups (veterans, abuse/assault survivors, first responders); connect to resources + structured follow-up; safety planning + means-restriction. PROTECTIVE: supportive partner, engagement in care, evidence-based treatment, peer support, reduced alcohol, coping skills. NEGATIVES (correct practice): no forced trauma narrative, no minimizing, no long-term benzos, no alcohol-coping advice, no surprising/cornering. Trauma-informed, nonjudgmental, recovery-oriented care.
providerOrders: SETTING: outpatient mental-health/primary-care clinic; quiet, low-stimulation room with accessible exit and non-cornering seating
DIAGNOSIS: post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) — combat-related; assess for comorbid depression and alcohol/substance use disorder
CODE STATUS / GOAL: establish safety, build a trauma-informed therapeutic relationship, teach grounding/coping, and connect to evidence-based treatment + recovery resources
SAFETY (FIRST):
Screen for suicidal/self-harm ideation, intent, and plan; complete a structured suicide-risk assessment; safety-plan as indicated
Screen alcohol/substance use; assess lethal-means access (firearms) and counsel means-restriction; assess current safety (e.g., intimate-partner violence)
Use validated tools as appropriate (e.g., PCL-5 for PTSD symptom severity; depression and substance screens)
TRAUMA-INFORMED CARE (all interactions):
Provide physical + emotional safety; explain before any procedure; offer choices; respect personal space; keep an accessible exit; avoid surprising or cornering
Calm, nonjudgmental, validating communication; let the client set the pace; do NOT force a detailed trauma narrative before he is ready; avoid minimizing statements
GROUNDING / FLASHBACK MANAGEMENT:
For flashback/dissociation/acute anxiety: speak calmly and orient to the present (safe, here, today, the event is over); use the 5-4-3-2-1 sensory method and paced/diaphragmatic breathing
Teach the client to recognize early cues and self-apply grounding and breathing between visits; avoid confrontation or restraint
TREATMENT (collaborative, choice-based):
Refer for trauma-focused psychotherapy — Cognitive Processing Therapy, Prolonged Exposure, Trauma-Focused CBT, or EMDR (first-line)
First-line medication: SSRI (sertraline or paroxetine) or SNRI (venlafaxine); set expectation of several weeks to full effect; reinforce adherence and follow-up
Prazosin for trauma-related nightmares/sleep disturbance (monitor for orthostatic hypotension/dizziness, especially first dose)
AVOID long-term/scheduled benzodiazepines; counsel reduction of alcohol and caffeine
HEALTH PROMOTION / RECOVERY:
Psychoeducation normalizing symptoms as a treatable response to trauma (reduce shame/self-blame, support engagement); sleep hygiene; regular exercise; relaxation/stress management
Connect to peer/veterans' support, mental-health and veterans' resources, and crisis lines; support gradual return to valued activities/work/relationships
FOLLOW-UP:
Structured mental-health follow-up; coordinate with psychiatry/prescriber, therapist, peer-support specialist, and social work; reassess symptoms, function, safety, and medication response

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters for this veteran presenting with possible PTSD?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: Intrusion symptoms — recurrent nightmares, intrusive memories, and flashbacks (dissociative reliving) with intense distress to trauma-related cues (loud noises, crowds)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — intrusion is a core DSM-5 PTSD cluster. RN role: recognize intrusion symptoms.

opts.text: Avoidance — he refuses to drive highways and avoids news, crowds, and reminders of the event
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — avoidance is a core DSM-5 PTSD cluster. RN role: recognize avoidance.

opts.text: Negative alterations in cognition/mood AND arousal — persistent guilt, detachment from family, irritability, hypervigilance, exaggerated startle, poor concentration, and severe insomnia, lasting >1 month with impairment
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — negative cognition/mood + arousal clusters, >1 month with impairment, complete the PTSD picture (vs <1 month = acute stress disorder). RN role: recognize all four clusters + timeframe.

opts.text: SAFETY — screen for suicidal/self-harm ideation, alcohol/substance use, and lethal-means access (firearms) and current safety, given PTSD's high comorbidity
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — safety/suicide/substance comorbidity must be screened FIRST. RN role: screen safety first.

opts.text: A trauma-informed approach is needed — give control and choice, explain before doing, avoid surprising/cornering, and be nonjudgmental
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — trauma-informed care frames all interactions and avoids re-traumatization. RN role: deliver trauma-informed care.

opts.text: He needs grounding/coping skills for flashbacks and hyperarousal (orient to present, 5-4-3-2-1, paced breathing) and connection to evidence-based treatment
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — grounding + linkage to trauma-focused therapy/medication are central nursing actions. RN role: teach grounding + connect to treatment.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — document and proceed
opts.rat: Allergy documentation; routine before any medication. Not the central cue.

opts.text: Pressure him to recount every detail of the traumatic event in the first session 'to get it all out,' even as he becomes distressed
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Forcing a detailed trauma narrative before the client is ready can RE-TRAUMATIZE him; let him set the pace and build safety/trust first.

opts.text: Since he 'survived and looks fine,' reassure him the symptoms aren't real and tell him to just move on
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Minimizing/dismissing symptoms is invalidating and erodes trust; PTSD is real and treatable. Validate and provide trauma-informed support.

opts.text: PTSD develops after exposure to actual/threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violence and features four DSM-5 clusters (intrusion, avoidance, negative cognition/mood, arousal/reactivity) lasting >1 month
opts.rat: CORRECT — etiology + the four clusters + timeframe. Foundation for recognition.

opts.text: Flashbacks, dissociation, and nightmares are intrusion symptoms; grounding (orient to the present, 5-4-3-2-1, paced breathing) helps reconnect the client to the here-and-now
opts.rat: CORRECT — intrusion phenomena + grounding response. Foundation for managing flashbacks.

opts.text: PTSD has high comorbidity with depression, substance/alcohol use disorder, and suicide — so safety and comorbidity screening come first
opts.rat: CORRECT — comorbidity/safety principle. Foundation for safety-first care.

opts.text: First-line treatment is trauma-focused psychotherapy (TF-CBT, Prolonged Exposure, CPT, EMDR); first-line medications are SSRIs/SNRIs, with prazosin for nightmares
opts.rat: CORRECT — evidence-based treatment. Foundation for connecting to care.

opts.text: Benzodiazepines are generally avoided in PTSD — they don't treat the core disorder, carry dependence/misuse risk (especially with comorbid substance use), and may worsen outcomes
opts.rat: CORRECT — benzodiazepine caution. Foundation for safe pharmacologic choices.

opts.text: Trauma-informed care (SAMHSA: safety, trust, peer support, collaboration, empowerment/choice, cultural sensitivity) avoids re-traumatization and gives the client control and predictability
opts.rat: CORRECT — trauma-informed-care principle. Foundation for every interaction.

opts.text: Avoidance and hypervigilance are simply character weakness that resolve with willpower, so no treatment is needed
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + stigmatizing. PTSD is a treatable disorder, not weakness; symptoms respond to trauma-focused therapy and appropriate medication.

opts.text: Long-term benzodiazepines are the preferred first-line treatment for PTSD and should be started immediately
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Benzodiazepines are generally AVOIDED in PTSD; first-line is trauma-focused psychotherapy +/- an SSRI/SNRI (prazosin for nightmares).

opts.text: Establish safety — screen for and address suicidal/self-harm risk, substance use, lethal-means access, and current danger
opts.rat: #1 — Safety is always first; PTSD's high suicide/substance comorbidity makes risk assessment the priority. RN role: safety first.

opts.text: Build a trauma-informed, nonjudgmental therapeutic relationship — provide safety, trust, control, choice, and predictability
opts.rat: #2 — High. A trusting, trauma-informed relationship enables all further assessment and care and avoids re-traumatization. RN role: therapeutic relationship.

opts.text: Teach grounding/coping skills for flashbacks and hyperarousal and help stabilize sleep
opts.rat: #3 — Stabilizing. Grounding and sleep give the client immediate, usable tools to manage symptoms. RN role: coping skills + sleep.

opts.text: Connect him to evidence-based treatment — trauma-focused psychotherapy and appropriate medication (SSRI/SNRI; prazosin for nightmares)
opts.rat: #4 — Definitive treatment. Linkage to therapy/medication drives recovery once safety and rapport are established. RN role: connect to treatment.

opts.text: Provide health promotion/recovery support — psychoeducation, peer/veterans' support, exercise, limiting alcohol, and follow-up/resources
opts.rat: #5 — Ongoing recovery. Psychoeducation, peer support, and lifestyle/follow-up sustain long-term outcomes. RN role: recovery support.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this veteran with PTSD.

opts.text: Screen for suicidal/self-harm ideation, substance/alcohol use, and lethal-means access (firearms), and assess current safety, before deeper trauma work
opts.rat: Safety-first screening for a high-comorbidity disorder. RN role: assess safety/suicide/substance use first.

opts.text: Use a trauma-informed approach — explain before doing, offer choices, respect personal space, keep an accessible exit, and remain calm and nonjudgmental while letting him set the pace
opts.rat: Core trauma-informed care that avoids re-traumatization. RN role: trauma-informed interaction.

opts.text: Teach grounding (orient to the present, 5-4-3-2-1 sensory method, paced breathing) for flashbacks, and validate that his symptoms are an understandable response to trauma
opts.rat: Grounding + validation manage intrusion/arousal and reduce shame. RN role: teach coping + validate.

opts.text: Refer for trauma-focused psychotherapy (CPT/PE/EMDR) and collaborate on first-line medication (SSRI/SNRI), with prazosin for nightmares
opts.rat: Evidence-based, first-line treatment. RN role: connect to therapy + appropriate medication.

opts.text: Provide psychoeducation, connect him to a veterans' peer-support group, and counsel sleep hygiene, exercise, and limiting alcohol/caffeine, with structured follow-up
opts.rat: Health promotion/recovery support sustains outcomes. RN role: psychoeducation + peer support + lifestyle + follow-up.

opts.text: Force him to recount the full trauma narrative in detail at the first contact regardless of his distress
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — a forced, premature trauma narrative can re-traumatize; let him set the pace after safety and trust are established.

opts.text: Start scheduled long-term benzodiazepines as the primary, first-line treatment for his PTSD
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — benzodiazepines don't treat core PTSD, risk dependence/misuse (especially with comorbid substance use), and may worsen outcomes; use trauma-focused therapy +/- SSRI/SNRI.

opts.text: Minimize and dismiss his symptoms ('you're fine, just move on') and discourage him from talking about what happened
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — minimizing is invalidating, erodes trust, and discourages care; validate and provide trauma-informed support.

opts.text: Approach him from behind without warning, corner him in a small room with no clear exit, and deliberately startle him to 'test' his reaction
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — surprising/cornering and provoking startle are re-traumatizing and unsafe; provide predictability, space, and an accessible exit.

opts.text: Advise him to manage his symptoms and insomnia by drinking alcohol to relax and fall asleep
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — alcohol worsens PTSD symptoms, sleep, and risk and fuels comorbid substance use; counsel reduction and teach healthy coping.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Assess and ensure safety first — screen for suicidal/self-harm ideation, substance use, lethal-means access, and current danger, and safety-plan as needed
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Safety/suicide-risk assessment precedes all other care in a high-comorbidity disorder. RN role: safety first.

opts.text: Establish a trauma-informed therapeutic relationship — calm, nonjudgmental, giving control/choice, explaining before doing, and letting him set the pace
opts.rat: #2 — Second. A trusting, trauma-informed relationship enables assessment and treatment and avoids re-traumatization. RN role: therapeutic relationship.

opts.text: Teach and practice grounding/coping for flashbacks and hyperarousal (orient to present, 5-4-3-2-1, paced breathing) and address sleep
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Grounding and sleep stabilization give immediate, usable tools. RN role: coping skills + sleep.

opts.text: Facilitate referral to trauma-focused psychotherapy and collaborate on medication (SSRI/SNRI; prazosin for nightmares), avoiding long-term benzodiazepines
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Linkage to definitive, evidence-based treatment drives recovery. RN role: connect to therapy + appropriate medication.

opts.text: Provide psychoeducation, connect to peer/veterans' support and resources, reinforce healthy coping, and arrange structured follow-up
opts.rat: #5 — Ongoing/recovery. Psychoeducation, peer support, and follow-up sustain long-term recovery. RN role: recovery support + follow-up.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: After learning the 5-4-3-2-1 grounding method and paced breathing, he aborts a flashback in clinic, reorients to the present, and his heart rate and blood pressure settle
opts.rat: Grounding successfully manages a flashback and hyperarousal. Expected, desired response.

opts.text: Started on sertraline with prazosin for nightmares; over several weeks his sleep improves and intrusive symptoms and nightmares lessen
opts.rat: Appropriate first-line SSRI + prazosin for nightmares taking effect over weeks. Improving trajectory.

opts.text: He engages in trauma-focused therapy (CPT) and a veterans' peer-support group; avoidance decreases and function at work and home improves
opts.rat: Engagement in first-line trauma-focused therapy + peer support driving recovery. Improving.

opts.text: Psychoeducation reframes his symptoms as a treatable response to trauma; his self-blame and shame decrease and he stays engaged in care
opts.rat: Psychoeducation reduces shame and supports engagement. Improving trajectory.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — a clinician forced a detailed trauma retelling at the first visit; he became acutely distressed, dissociated, and dropped out of care
opts.rat: ⚠️ A forced, premature trauma narrative re-traumatized him and disrupted care. Demonstrates letting the client set the pace after safety/trust.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — he was started on long-term scheduled benzodiazepines; he developed dependence and worse sleep and began combining them with alcohol
opts.rat: ⚠️ Long-term benzodiazepines fostered dependence and combined dangerously with alcohol. Demonstrates why benzodiazepines are avoided in PTSD.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — his symptoms were dismissed as weakness and left untreated; he escalated alcohol use and developed suicidal ideation
opts.rat: ⚠️ Dismissing/minimizing and failing to treat led to escalating substance use and suicide risk. Demonstrates the need for validation, treatment, and safety monitoring.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — staff repeatedly surprised and cornered him and dismissed his need for predictability; his hypervigilance and mistrust worsened and he stopped attending
opts.rat: ⚠️ Non-trauma-informed handling (surprising/cornering, removing control) re-traumatized him and broke the therapeutic relationship. Demonstrates trauma-informed care.

opts.text: Early in care, before therapy and medication take effect, his symptoms are present and essentially unchanged while rapport, safety, and coping skills are being built
opts.rat: Before trauma-focused therapy and medication take effect, an unchanged baseline is expected; continue building safety, rapport, and coping and reassess.

opts.text: At follow-up he reports restored sleep, sustained therapy engagement, reduced symptoms, lower alcohol use, and reconnection with his family and supports
opts.rat: Sustained recovery with treatment engagement, symptom reduction, and reconnection. Ideal longer-term outcome.

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Health Promotion and Maintenance, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
